components.functionalRole: Upper (series) resistor of the divider — sets the source impedance seen by the load and, together with R2, the divide ratio.
Lower (shunt) resistor to ground — fixes the output node voltage at Vin × R2 / (R1 + R2).
Tap point between R1 and R2 — feeds the downstream high-impedance load (ADC, op-amp input, comparator).

components.summary: Two equal resistors in series between +5 V and ground, with the output tap at their junction. Classic 2:1 voltage divider.

stages.name: Reference rail
stages.description: Stiff DC source supplies current into the series resistor.

stages.name: Series element
stages.description: R1 carries the divider current to the tap node.

stages.name: Shunt element
stages.description: R2 sinks the divider current to ground, fixing the tap voltage.

feedbackPaths.description: No feedback — open-loop passive network. Output depends entirely on the divider ratio and the impedance of the downstream load.

topology.powerSupplyTopology: Single-ended +5 V referenced to GND. No bypass.

domain.specificApplication: Reference / bias generation. Used to derive a stable mid-rail voltage, scale a signal into an ADC range, or set a bias point for a transistor or op-amp.
domain.frequencyRange: DC to ~1 MHz (limited by stray capacitance at the tap node)
domain.impedance: Source impedance at VOUT = R1 ∥ R2 = 5 kΩ
domain.industryContext: Universal — appears in every analog front end ever designed. The first building block in any electronics curriculum and the unsung hero of bandgap references, level translators, and feedback networks.

synthesis.operatingPrinciple: Two resistors in series form a current path from Vin to ground; the voltage at their midpoint equals the input scaled by the ratio R2 / (R1 + R2). With R1 = R2, the output is exactly Vin / 2. The divider only behaves ideally when the load impedance is much larger than the parallel combination of R1 and R2 — otherwise the load draws current that pulls the tap voltage down and changes the effective ratio.

keyParameters.name: Divide ratio
keyParameters.notes: R2 / (R1 + R2) — output is half the input

keyParameters.name: Quiescent current
keyParameters.notes: 5 V / 20 kΩ — wasted as heat in the divider

keyParameters.name: Bandwidth limit
keyParameters.notes: Approximate, set by ~10 pF of stray capacitance

synthesis.designDecisions: Choosing 10 kΩ for both resistors is the standard compromise between two competing failures: too small and you waste current (1 kΩ each would burn 2.5 mA continuously); too large and the source impedance starts to interact with ADC sampling capacitors, op-amp input bias currents, and stray PCB capacitance. 10 kΩ keeps the bleed current under a quarter milliamp while staying comfortably below the ~100 kΩ region where leakage and op-amp bias currents start to dominate. Using 1% metal-film resistors keeps the ratio error under ~1.4% worst case.

failureModes.scenario: Loading the output with a low-impedance device
failureModes.description: If the downstream stage has input impedance comparable to 5 kΩ (e.g. a bipolar op-amp's bias current path, or a relay coil), the tap voltage sags and the divide ratio is no longer R2 / (R1 + R2). The output collapses toward ground.

failureModes.scenario: Driving a switching capacitive load (ADC SAR)
failureModes.description: An ADC's sample-and-hold capacitor wants to charge through the 5 kΩ source impedance every conversion. If the ADC's acquisition time is shorter than ~5 × R × C_sample, the divider can't keep up and the reading is wrong.
5% carbon-film resistors can give a divide ratio anywhere from 0.476 to 0.526 in the worst case — enough error to matter for any precision application.

improvements.suggestion: Add a unity-gain op-amp buffer (e.g. OPA340) between the tap and the load.
improvements.rationale: Drops the effective source impedance from 5 kΩ to a few milliohms, isolating the divider from anything the load does. Essential if the load is variable or capacitive.

improvements.suggestion: Place a 100 nF ceramic from VOUT to ground.
improvements.rationale: Filters high-frequency noise on the input rail and gives ADC sample-and-hold circuits a local charge reservoir, fixing the acquisition-time problem above.

improvements.suggestion: Use a single matched resistor network (e.g. Vishay MORN, 0.1% ratio match) instead of two discrete resistors.
improvements.rationale: Ratio accuracy goes from ~1.4% to ~0.1%, and temperature drift tracks because both elements are on the same die.
